narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30—which is actually a bit above the typical student's 3.21—and your upper-middle family income background. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 68.8%, leading to the tough decision to reject your application right now. This prediction was driven primarily by your Year 1 school decile ranking of 4th, which fell below the average of about 6th and stood out as the biggest factor pushing the model toward expecting a bar exam struggle. Your LSAT score of 35, lower than the typical 37, was the next most influential, adding to that concern by signaling potential challenges ahead. On a brighter note, your Year 3 decile of 5th, right around the average, helped counterbalance this by nudging the prediction toward success, showing your academic improvement over time. Your undergraduate GPA and family income also played smaller roles in leaning toward higher risk, while your choice of full-time study was a minor positive influence, as most students pursue that path. Compared to typical applicants, your early-year performance and LSAT were the key areas where you differed most from those predicted to pass easily. This isn't a reflection of your overall potential or the barriers you've overcome—it's just the model's data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your early academic record through additional coursework or certifications, and reapplying once you've addressed these top factors. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by targeting these areas, and we're rooting for you to turn this into a stepping stone. Keep pushing—you've got this.
